lessons.title: The Beam: How a Radar Knows Which Way
lessons.content:
You already know a radar sends out radio waves and listens for the echo, and that timing the echo tells you **how far** something is. But distance alone is not enough: you also want to know **which direction** the target is in - its **angle**. This lesson is about how a radar figures that out.

The key tool is the **antenna**, and the key idea is the **beam**. Think of a bare light bulb versus a flashlight. A bare bulb throws light everywhere and lights nothing brightly. A flashlight has a reflector that **concentrates** the same light into a tight beam you can aim. An antenna does exactly this with radio waves: it concentrates the radar's energy into a **beam** instead of spraying it in all directions. (You met this in the range equation - a more focused antenna helps range.)

Now the clever part. Because the energy goes out in a beam pointing one way, when an echo comes back the radar knows it must have come from **wherever the beam was pointing**. That is how a radar measures **angle**: not by the echo itself, but by **which direction the beam was aimed** when the echo returned. Picture a searchlight sweeping the night sky, or a lighthouse beam turning - you know where the light is hitting because you know where the beam points.

This is why a **narrow** beam is so valuable. A wide beam is like a fat flashlight spot: the echo could be anywhere inside it, so you only roughly know the direction (poor **angular resolution** - the ability to tell apart directions). A narrow beam is like a tight spot: the echo's direction is pinned down precisely. And what makes a beam narrow? A **bigger antenna** - bigger compared to the **wavelength** (the length of one radio wave). The larger the antenna relative to the wavelength, the tighter the beam.

In short: an antenna concentrates energy into a beam, the radar reads a target's angle from the direction the beam is pointing, and a bigger antenna makes a narrower beam that measures that angle more precisely.

items.prompt: What does a radar antenna do with the radar's energy?
choices: It spreads the energy evenly in every direction at once
It concentrates the energy into a beam aimed in one direction, like a flashlight
It turns the energy into sound
It stores the energy until an echo returns
items.explanation: An antenna focuses the radar's energy into a beam pointing one way, just as a flashlight's reflector concentrates light, instead of a bare bulb spraying it everywhere. It does not radiate equally in all directions, turn energy into sound, or store it.

items.prompt: How does a radar figure out a target's ANGLE (which direction it is in)?
choices: From how long the echo takes to come back
From the colour of the echo
From which direction the beam was pointing when the echo came back
From how loud the transmitter is
items.explanation: The radar knows the echo came from wherever its beam was aimed, so the beam's pointing direction gives the target's angle, like knowing where a searchlight is hitting. Echo travel time gives distance (not angle), and colour or transmitter loudness do not indicate direction.

items.prompt: Which kind of beam lets a radar measure a target's direction MORE precisely?
choices: A narrow beam, because the echo can only come from a small range of directions
A wide beam, because it covers more sky
A wide beam, because wider always means more accurate
Beam width makes no difference to angle accuracy
items.explanation: A narrow beam confines the echo to a small set of directions, so you know the angle precisely (good angular resolution). A wide beam covers more sky but leaves the direction fuzzy; thinking wider gives better angle accuracy is the common mistake.

items.prompt: A beginner says: 'A wider beam gives better angular resolution.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Because a wider beam actually measures distance, not angle
Because a wider beam leaves the echo's direction fuzzy, so the angle is known less precisely
Because a wider beam cannot detect anything at all
Because beam width only matters at night
items.explanation: A wide beam means the echo could be anywhere inside that broad spread, so the direction is fuzzy and angular resolution is worse, not better. The narrow beam is the one that pins down angle. Beam width is about direction, works day or night, and a wide beam can still detect things.

items.prompt: What makes a radar's beam NARROWER?
choices: A smaller antenna
A bigger antenna (bigger compared with the wavelength)
Turning up the transmit power
Painting the antenna a darker colour
items.explanation: A larger antenna, measured against the wavelength, focuses energy into a tighter beam. A smaller antenna gives a wider beam; turning up power makes the beam stronger but not narrower, and colour has no effect on beam width.

items.prompt: A searchlight sweeping the night sky, or a lighthouse beam turning, is a good everyday picture of:
choices: How a radar measures a target's exact weight
How a radar aims a beam and learns direction from where the beam points
How a radar counts how many pulses it has sent
How a radar changes its transmit power
items.explanation: Like a searchlight or lighthouse, the radar aims its beam and knows the target's direction from where the beam is pointing. It has nothing to do with weighing targets, counting pulses, or changing power.

items.prompt: Why is a bare light bulb a good picture of a poor antenna, and a flashlight a good picture of a useful one?
choices: A bulb sprays light everywhere (no clear direction); a flashlight concentrates it into an aimable beam
A bulb is brighter than any flashlight could ever be
A flashlight spreads light in all directions, unlike a bulb
Neither has anything to do with how antennas work
items.explanation: A bare bulb throws energy in all directions so you cannot tell where anything is, while a flashlight concentrates it into a beam you can aim, which is exactly what a good antenna does with radio energy. The flashlight is the focused one, not the spreader.

items.prompt: What does 'angular resolution' mean for a radar?
choices: How far away the radar can detect a target
How precisely the radar can tell apart directions (angles)
How many colours the display can show
How fast the antenna spins
items.explanation: Angular resolution is how finely the radar can distinguish directions, which a narrow beam improves. It is not about detection range, display colours, or spin rate.

items.prompt: Two radars look at the same target. Radar A has a narrow beam, radar B a wide beam. Which one reports the target's direction more precisely?
choices: Radar B, the wide beam
They report direction equally well
Radar A, the narrow beam
Neither can report direction at all
items.explanation: The narrow-beam radar (A) confines the echo to a small set of directions, so it pins down the angle better. The wide-beam radar (B) leaves direction fuzzy. Both can sense direction, but not equally well.

items.prompt: The radar range equation taught that a more focused antenna helps range. Here we add a second job for that focus. What is it?
choices: It also measures the target's direction (angle) precisely
It also makes the echo travel faster
It also changes the target's size
It also removes the need for a receiver
items.explanation: A focused (narrow) beam both concentrates energy for range and pins down the target's direction for a precise angle reading. It does not speed up the echo, change the target, or replace the receiver.

lessons.title: Side Lobes, and Sweeping a Dish the Old Way
lessons.content:
A beam sounds tidy - a single clean spear of energy. Real beams are messier, and a radar must also **sweep** its beam around to search. This lesson covers both: the messiness (**side lobes**) and the classic way to sweep (**mechanical scanning**).

First, side lobes. A real antenna does send most of its energy into one strong beam - the **main lobe** - but it cannot help leaking a little energy in other directions too. Those weaker leftover beams are called **side lobes**. Think of a flashlight with a bright central spot plus faint glow off to the sides. The main lobe is where you are looking; the side lobes are small and unwanted. Why care? Because a side lobe can let in an echo, or clutter, or jamming from the **wrong** direction, and the radar might wrongly credit it to the main beam - a false angle reading. (Recall sub-topic 10: keeping side lobes low helps shut out jamming through the sides.) So designers work to keep side lobes as low as they can. For now, hold one picture: a big **main lobe** plus small **unwanted side lobes**.

Now, sweeping. One narrow beam only looks one way at a time, but you usually need to search all around. The old, simple answer is **mechanical scanning**: physically **rotate or tilt the antenna** so the beam sweeps the sky. This is the spinning dish you see at an airport or on a ship's mast, going round and round. It works and it is simple.

But it has real limits, all coming from the fact that you are **moving heavy metal**. You can only look **where the dish currently points** - to check another direction you must wait for the dish to swing there. Because the antenna is heavy, it can only turn so fast, so **revisiting** a target or switching to a new one is **slow**. Picture having to turn your whole head (and body) to look around a room: you simply cannot snap your gaze instantly from one corner to another.

In short: a real beam has a strong main lobe plus weak unwanted side lobes (kept low on purpose), and the traditional way to sweep the beam is to mechanically spin or tilt the dish - simple, but slow and limited because heavy metal must move.

items.prompt: What are 'side lobes'?
choices: The single strong beam where the antenna sends most of its energy
Weaker leftover beams of energy pointing in other, unwanted directions
The echoes that bounce off the ground
The buttons on the side of the radar console
items.explanation: Side lobes are the weaker beams of energy an antenna leaks in directions other than the main beam. The single strong beam is the main lobe, ground echoes are clutter, and buttons are not lobes.

items.prompt: What is the 'main lobe' of a beam?
choices: The strong central beam carrying most of the energy, where the radar is looking
A faint beam leaking out to the side
The noise inside the receiver
The power cable feeding the antenna
items.explanation: The main lobe is the strong central beam holding most of the energy, aimed where the radar wants to look. The faint side beams are side lobes; receiver noise and cables are unrelated.

items.prompt: A beginner mixes up the main lobe and the side lobes. Which statement is correct?
choices: The side lobes carry most of the energy and the main lobe is the faint one
The main lobe and side lobes are equally strong
The main lobe is the strong beam you aim; the side lobes are weak, unwanted beams pointing elsewhere
Only the side lobes can detect targets
items.explanation: The main lobe is the strong, aimed beam; the side lobes are the weak, unwanted leakage in other directions. They are not equal, the side lobes are not the strong ones, and detection is normally done with the main lobe.

items.prompt: Why do radar designers try to keep side lobes LOW?
choices: Because side lobes can let in echoes, clutter, or jamming from the wrong direction and cause false angle readings
Because side lobes make the radar too quiet to hear
Because low side lobes make the antenna spin faster
Because side lobes change the speed of the echo
items.explanation: Low side lobes shut out unwanted echoes, clutter, and jamming arriving through the sides, and reduce false angle readings (a point from sub-topic 10). Side lobes do not affect sound, spin speed, or echo speed.

items.prompt: What is 'mechanical scanning'?
choices: Steering the beam electronically with no moving parts
Physically rotating or tilting the antenna so the beam sweeps around
Turning the transmitter on and off very fast
Painting the sky with a laser
items.explanation: Mechanical scanning means physically moving the antenna - rotating or tilting it - to sweep the beam, like a spinning airport dish. Steering with no moving parts is electronic (next lesson); it is not about toggling the transmitter or lasers.

items.prompt: The spinning dish on a ship's mast or at an airport, going round and round, is an example of:
choices: A phased array with no moving parts
Mechanical scanning (physically moving the antenna to sweep the beam)
A side lobe
A receiver filter
items.explanation: A dish physically spinning to sweep its beam is mechanical scanning. A phased array specifically has no moving parts, a side lobe is a stray beam, and a receiver filter is signal processing - none of those is the spinning dish.

items.prompt: What is the main DOWNSIDE of mechanical scanning?
choices: It cannot detect any targets at all
It works only on rainy days
Moving heavy metal is slow, so you can only look where the dish points now and revisiting is slow
It uses no power
items.explanation: Because a heavy antenna must physically swing around, you can only look where it currently points, and revisiting or switching targets is slow. It can certainly detect targets, works in any weather, and does use power.

items.prompt: Having to turn your whole head and body to look around a room is a good everyday picture of:
choices: Mechanical scanning, where moving the heavy antenna limits how fast you can switch where you look
Electronic steering, which is instant
How a side lobe forms
How an echo's distance is measured
items.explanation: Turning your whole head to look around - slow, one direction at a time - mirrors mechanical scanning, where moving metal limits how fast you can re-aim. Electronic steering is the instant alternative, not this analogy.

items.prompt: With a mechanically scanned dish, why can't you instantly check a target behind you?
choices: Because radar waves cannot travel backwards
Because you must wait for the heavy dish to physically swing around to point there
Because the receiver is switched off behind the radar
Because targets behind you give no echo
items.explanation: The beam only points where the dish points, so you must wait for the heavy antenna to rotate around - the core limit of mechanical scanning. Radar waves can go any direction the antenna faces, and targets behind do echo once the dish turns to them.

items.prompt: Why might a side lobe cause a FALSE angle reading?
choices: Because an echo coming in through a side lobe may be credited to the main beam's direction
Because side lobes make the main beam point backwards
Because side lobes always travel faster than the main lobe
Because side lobes change the colour of the target
items.explanation: If energy from a real object enters through a side lobe, the radar may wrongly assume it came from where the main beam points, giving a false angle. Side lobes do not reverse or speed up the main beam, nor change target colour.

lessons.title: Phased Arrays: Steering the Beam by Timing
lessons.content:
Mechanical scanning works, but moving heavy metal is slow. The modern leap fixes that with **no moving parts at all**: the **phased array**.

Here is the idea. Instead of one dish you swing around, use **many small fixed antenna elements** packed onto a flat panel - dozens, hundreds, even thousands. None of them moves. Yet the combined beam can be pointed wherever you like, and re-pointed almost **instantly**. How? By controlling the **timing** of each element.

Think of a stadium crowd doing 'the wave'. Each person stands a moment after their neighbour; that little staggered timing makes a ripple **travel** along the stands in a chosen direction. Or picture a line of swimmers, each starting their kick a beat after the one before - the splash front angles off to one side. A phased array does the same with radio waves: it **delays each element a tiny bit** relative to its neighbour. (Engineers adjust the wave's **phase**, its timing within a cycle - hence 'phased' array.) When the delays are set just right, all the little waves **add up in step** (reinforce) in one chosen direction and **cancel out of step** elsewhere. Change the pattern of delays and the combined beam **tilts** to a new angle - electronically, in a flash. This add-up-in-step idea is called **beamforming**, and it is the very same trick used in 5G phones and Wi-Fi. Important: beamforming changes the **direction** of the beam, not the transmit power.

Why do phased arrays win? **Instant steering**: the beam can hop between many directions in any order, so the radar can track many targets seemingly at once and interleave searching with tracking. **No mechanical wear**: nothing moves. **Multiple beams**: it can even form several beams at the same time. And it can steer a **null** - a deliberate blind spot - straight onto a jammer (recall sub-topic 10). One modern term: **AESA** (active electronically scanned array) means each element has its own tiny transmit/receive module - this is what modern fighter, weather, and automotive radars use. The trade-off, lightly: arrays are complex and costly, and steering the beam very far off straight-ahead makes it **widen** (a limited scan angle).

In short: a phased array uses many fixed elements and steers the beam by adjusting each element's timing so the waves add up in the chosen direction - no moving parts, near-instant re-pointing, multiple beams, and the ability to null a jammer.

items.prompt: How does a phased array point its beam in a different direction?
choices: It physically rotates the whole panel to face that way
It adjusts the timing (phase) of its many fixed elements so the waves add up in the chosen direction
It turns up the transmit power on one side
It waits for the wind to push the antenna around
items.explanation: A phased array steers electronically: by delaying each fixed element a little, the waves reinforce in one direction and cancel elsewhere, tilting the beam. Nothing physically rotates, and steering is about timing, not raising power on one side.

items.prompt: A beginner says: 'A phased array steers its beam by physically swinging the antenna around.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Because a phased array has no moving parts; it steers electronically by adjusting element timing
Because a phased array cannot change its beam direction at all
Because a phased array only works while spinning
Because a phased array steers by changing the wavelength of the radio waves
items.explanation: The whole point of a phased array is that nothing moves - it steers by adjusting the phase/timing of fixed elements. Thinking it physically moves is the classic misconception; it does change direction (electronically), does not spin, and steering is by timing, not by changing wavelength.

items.prompt: What is the KEY difference between mechanical scanning and a phased array?
choices: Mechanical scanning is electronic; a phased array moves metal
Mechanical scanning moves heavy metal to aim the beam; a phased array steers the beam electronically with no moving parts
There is no real difference; the names are interchangeable
Mechanical scanning works only in daylight; a phased array only at night
items.explanation: Mechanical scanning physically swings the antenna, while a phased array steers electronically with nothing moving - move metal versus steer electronically. The two are not the same, and neither is tied to time of day. (The first option reverses the two.)

items.prompt: A stadium crowd doing 'the wave', or a line of swimmers each starting a beat later, is a good everyday picture of:
choices: How staggered timing across elements steers a phased-array beam in a chosen direction
How a heavy dish is rotated to sweep its beam
How a receiver filters out noise
How an echo's distance is timed
items.explanation: The staggered timing that makes a stadium wave or a swimmers' splash front travel one way mirrors delaying each array element to steer the beam. It is not about rotating a dish, filtering noise, or timing distance.

items.prompt: In a phased array, when the element timings are set right, the many little waves:
choices: Add up in step (reinforce) in the chosen direction and cancel out of step elsewhere
All cancel everywhere, so nothing is transmitted
Add up equally in every direction at once
Turn into heat inside the panel
items.explanation: Correct timing makes the waves reinforce in the chosen direction and cancel elsewhere, which is what forms and aims the beam. They do not cancel everywhere, add up in all directions, or become heat.

items.prompt: A beginner thinks beamforming works by changing the transmit POWER to aim the beam. What does beamforming actually change?
choices: The colour of the radio waves
The timing (phase) of the elements, which changes the DIRECTION of the beam
The weight of the antenna
The distance to the target
items.explanation: Beamforming adjusts the relative timing/phase of the elements to set the beam's direction; it is about where the energy goes, not how much power is sent. It does not change colour, weight, or the target's distance.

items.prompt: A beginner claims: 'Electronic steering is slower than mechanically swinging a dish.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Because electronic steering also requires moving the panel
Because mechanical dishes actually move at the speed of light
Because electronic steering has no moving parts, so the beam can be re-pointed almost instantly - far faster than swinging metal
Because electronic steering cannot change direction at all
items.explanation: With nothing to physically move, a phased array re-points its beam almost instantly, far faster than a heavy dish can swing - so electronic steering is faster, not slower. The panel does not move, dishes are limited by mechanical speed, and electronic steering certainly does change direction.

items.prompt: Which of these is a genuine ADVANTAGE of a phased array?
choices: It can instantly re-point to track many targets, form multiple beams, and steer a null onto a jammer
It is always far cheaper and simpler than a dish
It keeps its beam perfectly narrow no matter how far off-centre it steers
It needs no electronics at all
items.explanation: Phased arrays shine at instant re-pointing (tracking many targets, interleaving search and track), forming multiple beams, and nulling a jammer. They are actually complex and costly, the beam widens when steered far off-centre, and they rely heavily on electronics.

items.prompt: How can a phased array help against a jammer (recall sub-topic 10)?
choices: By spinning faster than the jammer
By steering a null - a deliberate blind spot - onto the jammer so its energy is largely ignored
By painting the jammer on the display
By turning the jammer's signal into a target
items.explanation: Because it controls element timing, a phased array can place a null (blind spot) in the jammer's direction, ignoring most of its energy - the spatial nulling idea from sub-topic 10. It does not spin (nothing moves), and it cannot recolour or convert the jammer's signal into a real target.

items.prompt: What does 'AESA' (active electronically scanned array) mean in plain terms?
choices: A dish that spins twice as fast as normal
A phased array where each element has its own tiny transmit/receive module
A radar that uses no antenna at all
A way of painting the radar to reduce its echo
items.explanation: AESA means an electronically steered array in which every element has its own little transmit/receive module - used by modern fighter, weather, and automotive radars. It is not a fast-spinning dish, an antenna-less radar, or a paint coating.

items.prompt: What is a light trade-off (downside) of phased arrays?
choices: They can only ever form one beam, like a dish
They are complex and costly, and steering far off straight-ahead makes the beam widen (limited scan angle)
They wear out faster than a spinning dish
They cannot steer the beam at all without moving
items.explanation: Phased arrays are complex and expensive, and as the beam is steered far from straight-ahead it broadens, limiting the useful scan angle. They can form multiple beams, have no moving parts to wear out, and steer without moving.

items.prompt: Pulling the sub-topic together: which statement best sums up how radars point and steer their beam?
choices: The antenna sets the beam that measures angle; old radars swept a dish mechanically, while modern radars use phased arrays to steer the beam electronically and instantly
Radars learn angle from echo colour and never need to steer a beam
All radars steer their beams by physically spinning, and phased arrays are just heavier dishes
Beam direction does not matter; only transmit power decides where a radar looks
items.explanation: The big picture: the antenna forms the beam that pins down angle; mechanical scanning swings a dish, while phased arrays steer electronically and near-instantly, enabling tracking many targets, multiple beams, and nulling. Angle does not come from colour, phased arrays do not move, and direction (not just power) decides where a radar looks.
